feedback.text: Hello, I find the idea of strengthening consumer protection on the internet very good. However, I think there is no aspect that protects the buyer from problems with the sale of licences. Sellers have the option to sell only one licence for one software, but not the software itself. This is often linked to the possibility for sellers to disable this licence at any time, even without any clear reason. In this way, users are often forced to switch to a new product that is almost identical to the old one. I have experienced several times that I bought software, such as a video game, only to discover later that it was deactivated by the seller. One concrete example is Overwatch, a game I bought and which was then inactivated. When I buy a video game or a program, I want to actually own the software and not just get a licence that allows me to use it but can be withdrawn at any time. Another strategy of sellers, which is also covered by this licensing and software problem, is to sell memory cards or similar data carriers where the buyer believes that the software is stored locally. In reality, however, the memory card contains only one product key. An example of this is Nintendo, which has now started to put memory cards in game packaging with a product key instead of memory cards with the actual software. It is a great concern for me to actually own the software I buy and not to acquire an access licence. I thank you for your attention.
